abstract: As the world’s most popular spectator sport, soccer is a global stage where diverse cultures converge around a single goal. Beyond the pitch, the game has evolved since the late nineteenth century into a profound social phenomenon. Scholars globally have long used soccer as a lens through which to examine the complexities of modern life, including its intersection with politics, economics, religion, media, and aesthetics. *Soccer & Society*, the first international journal dedicated exclusively to the sport, explores the game within the context of an increasingly globalized world. By integrating anthropological, historical, sociological, and economic perspectives, the journal provides an interdisciplinary forum for analyzing the deepening relationship between soccer and the vital structures of society. Committed to a truly international scope, *Soccer & Society* seeks to foster original empirical research from scholars across the globe, bridging the gap between sport and the human experience.
